one_line_takeaway: TabooRAG shows that because modern LLMs share highly overlapping safety-alignment refusal criteria, a single fabricated 'restricted risk context' document optimized against one accessible surrogate LLM can be transferred, without any target access, to reliably trigger refusals (denial-of-service) in unrelated black-box RAG systems, achieving up to 96.1% attack success while evading perplexity, paraphrasing, and Prompt-Guard defenses.
key_finding: TabooRAG, optimized against a single surrogate LLM (DeepSeek-V3.2) and transferred without any access to the target, achieves state-of-the-art blocking Attack Success Rate (ASR) across all seven tested target LLMs and three datasets, reaching up to 96.1% ASR on GPT-5.2/HotpotQA (paper's headline number, also stated as 'up to 96%' in the abstract). By contrast, prior blocking attacks collapse on modern safety-aligned models: MuteRAG's ASR falls as low as 0.8-0.9% on GPT-5 series models (Table 1/Table 3), and Jamming Attack likewise trails far behind on the newer models, despite both achieving comparable or higher retrieval recall (Table 3) -- demonstrating the gap is in refusal-inducing capability, not retrievability. Cross-model transfer holds even from small 8B-scale surrogates: documents optimized against Llama-3-8B reach 81.9% ASR when transferred to GPT-5.2 (Table 2). The strategy library reduces required optimization iterations by ~70-80% (Fig. 2) and enables 'warm-start' transfer of strategies from a strong attacker (GPT-5.2) to weaker attacker LLMs, improving their ASR by ~9-19 percentage points (Table 6: Qwen-3-32B +9.2%, Gemma-3-12B +19.1%, GPT-5-mini +8.9%). TabooRAG is also far cheaper than the prior black-box Jamming Attack in query/token cost (8.9 LLM calls / 16,497 tokens per document vs. 3,872 calls / 3,469,312 tokens for Jamming Attack; Table 8), because it needs no repeated target-system probing. Against defenses, TabooRAG's perplexity distribution overlaps with clean documents (Fig. 4), evading GPT-2-based PPL filtering that catches Jamming/MuteRAG; paraphrasing defense only moderately suppresses ASR on MS-MARCO (68.9%->42.9%) and is ineffective on NQ/HotpotQA; and Meta's Prompt-Guard-86M is largely ineffective (Table 7) and on MS-MARCO even increases ASR (68.9%->71.4%) because it over-filters benign 'how-to' procedural queries as instructions, amplifying adversarial content's relative influence.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TabooRAG, a black-box, transferable 'blocking attack' against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Instead of using adversarial suffixes or explicit malicious instructions (which the authors show are increasingly ineffective against modern safety-aligned LLMs), TabooRAG generates a single retrievable document that wraps the user's benign query in a fabricated 'restricted risk context' (invented but plausible-sounding risk details, e.g. fake files/events/timestamps) written in an objective, Wikipedia/legal style. This causes the target LLM's own safety-alignment machinery to misclassify the benign query as unsafe and refuse to answer, achieving a denial-of-service effect. The attack is optimized entirely against an attacker-controlled surrogate LLM/RAG environment (never touching the real target) and is then transferred directly to unseen target systems. The paper's central claim, which it terms 'alignment homogeneity,' is that because mainstream LLM safety-alignment practices share overlapping risk categories and refusal criteria, a restricted-risk-context document built against one accessible model transfers effectively to other, unrelated black-box models. A secondary contribution is a 'query-aware strategy library' that stores previously successful blocking strategies indexed by embeddings of query profiles (Topic/Intent/Keywords), reused as warm-start heuristics to reduce optimization iterations and to let strategies learned by a strong attacker LLM be reused by weaker attacker LLMs.
threat_model: Black-box RAG availability attack. System: retriever R + generator G over an open-domain knowledge base that is 'partially open' (third parties can upload documents, e.g. Wikipedia/Reddit-style corpora). Attacker capabilities: cannot access the retriever, generator, or intermediate retrieval results; cannot modify existing documents; can inject exactly one document per query; cannot repeatedly probe the target system for feedback (to avoid detection). The attacker may use independent, accessible surrogate LLMs and a surrogate retriever to optimize documents offline, then transfers the finished document to the target system with no further interaction. Attacker goal: for a given benign query q, get the injected document retrieved (top-k) AND cause the generator to produce a refusal response, rather than an incorrect answer (this is explicitly an availability/denial-of-service attack, contrasted with 'misleading' attacks like PoisonedRAG that aim to produce a specific wrong answer).
limitations: The paper's own reported limitations/caveats: (1) ASR judging relies on an LLM judge (GPT-5.2) with human verification of false positive/negative rates reported only in an appendix not captured in this extraction, so judge reliability is not directly quantified here; (2) retrievability (recall) for TabooRAG is somewhat lower than some baselines on NQ/MS-MARCO (83.1% and 70.5% vs. e.g. Jamming's 97.2%/90.1%), meaning the attack is not maximally optimized purely for retrieval, trading some recall for refusal-inducing potency; (3) the paper tests only three off-the-shelf defenses (perplexity filtering, paraphrasing, Prompt-Guard) and does not evaluate defenses specifically designed to counter this new restricted-risk-context attack surface, noting this 'reveal[s] a need for improved defenses' as future work rather than a solved problem; (4) the fabricated competitive context and strategy-preference analysis rely on the same attacker/judge LLM family (GPT-5.2) for much of the evaluation, which could introduce self-consistency bias; (5) the paper explicitly frames the attack surface as emerging from increasing standardization of safety alignment ('alignment homogeneity') but does not propose a concrete defense/mitigation, leaving the fix to future work.

datasets_used: HotpotQA (all from the BEIR benchmark suite, 150 sampled queries each)
quantitative_results: TabooRAG ASR on HotpotQA/GPT-5.2: 96.1% (±1.12%), the paper's headline result
MuteRAG ASR on GPT-5-mini (MS-MARCO): 0.8%; on GPT-5.2 (MS-MARCO): 0.9%
Cross-model transfer: documents optimized on Llama-3-8B achieve 81.9% ASR on GPT-5.2 (Table 2)

Retrieval recall: TabooRAG 83.1% (NQ), 70.5% (MS-MARCO), 100.0% (HotpotQA); ASR-vs-recall shows TabooRAG achieves higher ASR than PoisonedRAG at comparable recall using a single document instead of five
Strategy library reduces optimization iterations by approximately 70-80% (Fig. 2)
Warm-start strategy transfer gains: Qwen-3-32B +9.2%, Gemma-3-12B +19.1%, GPT-5-mini +8.9% ASR (Table 6)

Cost per document: TabooRAG 8.9 LLM calls / 16,497 tokens vs. Jamming Attack 3,872 calls / 3,469,312 tokens vs. PoisonedRAG 3.0 calls/1,108 tokens vs. AuthChain 6.7 calls/3,270 tokens vs. MuteRAG 0 calls/0 tokens (Table 8)
Defenses on TabooRAG (Table 7): w/o defense 77.1%/68.9%/96.1% (NQ/MS-MARCO/HotpotQA); Paraphrasing 73.5%/42.9%/96.1%; Prompt-Guard 77.1%/71.4%/82.4%
baselines_compared: Jamming Attack (blocking attack, Shafran et al.)
